questions.stem: A gardener develops itchy, vesicular streaks on the forearms 3 days after weeding, in a pattern matching where the plants brushed her skin. Which Gell and Coombs hypersensitivity type does this represent?
questions.explain: Allergic contact dermatitis (e.g. poison ivy) is a delayed type IV, T-cell–mediated reaction appearing over days; it is investigated with patch testing.

questions.stem: Ten minutes after a wasp sting, a 40-year-old develops generalized hives, throat tightness, wheeze, and a blood pressure of 80/50. What is the best immediate treatment?
options: Intramuscular epinephrine into the outer thigh
questions.explain: This is anaphylaxis. IM epinephrine into the outer thigh is first-line, repeated every 5–15 min as needed. Antihistamines, steroids, and bronchodilators are adjuncts that don't reverse airway obstruction or shock.

questions.stem: A patient in anaphylactic shock has already received an antihistamine and IV corticosteroid from a well-meaning bystander clinician, but remains hypotensive and wheezing. Why are these agents insufficient?
options: They are too low in dose; doubling them will reverse the shock
They do not reverse airway obstruction or hypotension — epinephrine is required and acts on those directly
Steroids work within 2 minutes and should have already worked
questions.explain: H1 blockers ease itch/hives and steroids may reduce biphasic reactions, but neither reverses bronchospasm or hypotension. Epinephrine is the only first-line agent — its α/β effects address airway and blood pressure.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old has daily itchy hives for 10 weeks; each individual lesion fades within 12 hours leaving no mark. There is no consistent food/drug trigger, and ESR/CRP are normal. First antihistamine at standard dose helps only partially. What is the next step?
options: Up-titrate the non-sedating H1 antihistamine (up to 4× standard dose); add omalizumab if refractory
Urgent skin biopsy and start colchicine
questions.explain: This is chronic spontaneous urticaria (>6 weeks, each lesion <24 h, no trigger, normal inflammatory markers). Management escalates a non-sedating H1 antihistamine up to 4×, then omalizumab for refractory disease.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man has recurrent episodes of tongue and hand swelling lasting 2–3 days with severe crampy abdominal pain, but never any itching or hives. Antihistamines, prednisone, and epinephrine have all failed. His father had similar attacks. C4 is undetectable. Which mediator and diagnosis fit?
questions.explain: Angioedema without hives, lasting days, with abdominal pain, family history, low C4, and no response to antihistamine/steroid/epinephrine is classic hereditary C1-inhibitor deficiency. The mediator is bradykinin, so histamine-directed therapy fails; treat with C1-INH replacement, a bradykinin-receptor blocker, or anti-kallikrein.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old started lisinopril 3 months ago and now presents with isolated lip and tongue swelling, no hives and no itch. What is the mechanism and the key management step?
options: Histamine release; give an antihistamine and continue lisinopril
Bradykinin accumulation; stop the ACE inhibitor and avoid the class
IgE-mediated drug allergy; desensitize to lisinopril
questions.explain: ACE-inhibitor angioedema is bradykinin-mediated (ACE normally degrades bradykinin), causes swelling without urticaria, and can occur months into therapy. Stop the ACE inhibitor and avoid the class; manage the airway.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old has recurrent hive-like lesions that burn rather than itch, each persisting 2–3 days and leaving brown discolouration, accompanied by arthralgia. Antihistamines barely help. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure — this is typical chronic spontaneous urticaria
Skin biopsy; this suggests urticarial vasculitis and systemic evaluation
questions.explain: Painful/burning lesions lasting >24 h with residual pigmentation and systemic features suggest urticarial vasculitis (type III immune-complex). Confirm with skin biopsy, evaluate for systemic involvement, and treat with immunosuppression — antihistamines respond poorly.

questions.stem: Five days after starting a new antibiotic, a patient develops isolated thrombocytopenia with mucosal bleeding; no rash, fever, or organ dysfunction. What hypersensitivity mechanism is most likely?
options: Type II IgG-mediated destruction of drug-coated platelets
questions.explain: Drug-induced cytopenias are a type II reaction — IgG against drug-coated platelets/RBCs/WBCs, typically 2–7 days after starting. Stop the drug and give supportive care.

questions.stem: Two weeks after a course of an antibiotic, a patient develops fever, an urticarial/morbilliform rash, arthralgias, and tender lymphadenopathy. Which reaction pattern is this?
options: Morbilliform exanthem with no systemic features
questions.explain: Delayed onset (1–3 weeks) with fever, rash, arthralgia, and lymphadenopathy fits serum sickness / serum-sickness-like reaction, a type III immune-complex process. Stop the trigger and treat symptomatically.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old presents with his second episode of Neisseria meningitidis meningitis. Which immune defect should be evaluated, and with what test?
questions.explain: Recurrent Neisseria infection points to a terminal complement (C5–C9) deficiency, screened with CH50/AH50. Manage with vaccination and prophylaxis.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old with SLE has nephritis, arthritis, a malar rash, low C3/C4, and positive anti-dsDNA. Which immunologic mechanism best explains the multi-organ involvement?
options: Type III immune-complex deposition with complement activation
Type IV cytotoxic T cells confined to the pancreas
questions.explain: SLE is a systemic type III disease: anti-nuclear autoantibodies form immune complexes that deposit in kidney, skin, joints, and serosa, activating complement (hence low C3/C4).

questions.stem: A patient with known SLE and prior lupus nephritis returns for monitoring. Which combination of labs best tracks disease/renal activity over time?
questions.explain: Anti-dsDNA and complement (C3/C4) track activity, especially nephritis (rising dsDNA, falling complement signal flare). ANA is not repeated to monitor, and anti-Sm doesn't follow activity well.

questions.stem: A woman with active SLE on prednisone and mycophenolate develops a new fever. Anti-dsDNA is mildly up and complement mildly down. What is the most important principle in her evaluation?
options: The fever is certainly a lupus flare; escalate immunosuppression immediately
Treat fever in an immunosuppressed SLE patient as infection until proven otherwise, while keeping flare and other causes in the differential
Fever is most likely a drug reaction to mycophenolate; stop it
Fever rules out infection because she is on steroids
questions.explain: In an SLE patient on prednisone/mycophenolate, fever is infection until proven otherwise — immunosuppression both raises infection risk and can mask it. Flare is possible (and the serologies hint at activity), but you must not escalate immunosuppression before excluding infection; also consider drug fever, thrombosis, and malignancy.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with lupus nephritis on mycophenolate and a prednisone taper wants to conceive in the next year. Which counselling is most accurate?
options: Continue mycophenolate — it is the safest agent in pregnancy
Mycophenolate is teratogenic and should be switched well before conception, aiming for disease quiescence; hydroxychloroquine is generally continued
Stop all medications immediately and conceive now
Pregnancy is low-risk as long as ANA is no longer followed
questions.explain: Mycophenolate is teratogenic — switch to a pregnancy-compatible agent (e.g. azathioprine) well before conception and aim for quiescence. Hydroxychloroquine is usually continued. Antiphospholipid antibodies, if present, add thrombosis/pregnancy-loss risk.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old has petechiae and isolated platelets of 12 × 10⁹/L with normal hemoglobin, WBC, PT, and aPTT. ITP is suspected. Which mechanism explains the low platelets?
options: IgG antibodies against platelet surface glycoproteins (e.g. GPIIb/IIIa)
questions.explain: ITP is a type II reaction: IgG against platelet glycoproteins (e.g. GPIIb/IIIa) drives destruction. Isolated thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation times fits platelet destruction, not a clotting-factor problem.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old has weight loss, tremor, heat intolerance, a diffuse goiter, and eye prominence. TSH is suppressed, free T4 elevated, and antibodies stimulate the TSH receptor. Which diagnosis and mechanism fit?
options: Graves disease; type II receptor-stimulating antibody
questions.explain: Graves disease is a type II reaction in which a TSH-receptor–stimulating IgG drives hyperthyroidism. Hashimoto instead causes hypothyroidism via destructive autoimmune thyroiditis.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old has recurrent hive-like rashes, 1–2 day fevers, shin/ankle pain, and progressive hearing loss. Infectious, malignant, and autoimmune work-ups are negative; ESR/CRP are high and genetics show an NLRP3 mutation. Which therapy targets the mechanism?
questions.explain: This is CAPS, an autoinflammatory disease: an NLRP3 mutation causes inappropriate inflammasome activation and excess IL-1β. Anti-IL-1 therapy (canakinumab/anakinra) directly targets the pathway.

questions.stem: A 2-month-old boy has failure to thrive, chronic diarrhea, oral thrush, and Pneumocystis pneumonia. HIV testing of infant and parents is negative; newborn screening showed absent TRECs. What is the diagnosis and a key management point?
options: Severe combined immunodeficiency; avoid live vaccines and arrange HSCT
questions.explain: FTT, thrush, PCP, and absent TRECs in infancy indicate SCID — a medical emergency. Avoid live vaccines, treat infections aggressively, and pursue hematopoietic stem cell transplant as early as possible.

questions.stem: A boy has recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections plus an episode of Pneumocystis pneumonia. Immunoglobulins show high-normal IgM with low IgG and IgA. Which defect best explains the full picture?
questions.explain: High IgM with low IgG/IgA = a hyper-IgM phenotype (no class switching). The added opportunistic infection (PCP) points to CD40L deficiency, because CD40L also drives macrophage activation. AID deficiency gives the same antibody pattern but spares macrophage function, so it lacks the opportunistic-infection susceptibility.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old woman has recurrent bacterial sinusitis, otitis, and two pneumonias, plus autoimmune thrombocytopenia. Quantitative immunoglobulins show low IgG and low IgA, and pneumococcal vaccine titres are poor. Which diagnosis and management fit best?
options: X-linked agammaglobulinemia, which usually first presents in adult women
questions.explain: Adult-onset recurrent sinopulmonary infections with low IgG plus low IgA/IgM, poor vaccine responses, and autoimmune cytopenias define CVID. Treat with immunoglobulin replacement and monitor for autoimmune, lung, and malignancy complications. (XLA presents in boys after 6 months, not adult women.)

cards.prompt: Match each CD4 helper subset to its signature:
cards.explain: Each subset's cytokine defines the infections seen when it fails.

cards.prompt: What interaction delivers T-cell help to B cells, and what does it enable?
cards.answer: CD40L (on T cell) binding CD40 (on B cell), plus cytokines — enabling proliferation, class switching, affinity maturation, and plasma/memory cell formation.

cards.text: Hyper-IgM shows {{high}} IgM with low {{IgG/IgA}}. CD40L deficiency also impairs {{macrophage}} activation, so it adds opportunistic infections like {{Pneumocystis}}; AID deficiency does not.
cards.explain: Class-switch failure; CD40L vs AID separated by opportunistic infection.

cards.prompt: Recurrent Neisseria infection most suggests a defect in:
cards.explain: Terminal complement (C5–C9) defects classically cause recurrent Neisseria infection.

cards.prompt: Match each Gell & Coombs type to an example:
cards.explain: I=IgE, II=antibody vs cell surface, III=immune complex, IV=T cell.

cards.prompt: Why is epinephrine — not antihistamines or steroids — first-line in anaphylaxis?
cards.answer: Only epinephrine reverses the airway obstruction and hypotension (via α/β effects). Antihistamines ease itch/hives and steroids may blunt biphasic reactions, but both are adjuncts that don't treat shock or bronchospasm.

cards.prompt: Match the drug reaction to its timing/type:
cards.explain: Timing and mechanism distinguish the drug-reaction patterns.

cards.prompt: How does the hypersensitivity mechanism guide allergy testing?
cards.answer: IgE (type I) disease → skin testing or serum specific IgE; type IV contact dermatitis → patch testing; severe type IV drug reactions → stop the drug and assess for systemic involvement.

cards.text: In urticaria each individual lesion resolves within {{24 hours}}; angioedema is deeper and resolves more slowly, up to about {{72 hours}}.
cards.explain: Lesion duration helps separate urticaria from angioedema and from vasculitis.

cards.prompt: Daily hives for >6 weeks, each lesion <24 h, no trigger, normal ESR/CRP. Diagnosis?
cards.explain: CSU: non-sedating H1 antihistamine, up-dose to 4×, omalizumab if refractory.

cards.prompt: Why do antihistamines, steroids, and epinephrine fail in C1-inhibitor deficiency?
cards.answer: Because the swelling is mediated by bradykinin, not histamine. Treat with C1-INH replacement, a bradykinin-receptor blocker (icatibant), or anti-kallikrein therapy.

cards.prompt: Which features point to bradykinin-mediated angioedema rather than allergic? (select all)
cards.explain: No hives, abdominal pain, days-long course, and no response to antihistamine/epi/steroid suggest HAE or ACE-inhibitor angioedema; check C4 and C1-INH.

cards.prompt: What distinguishes urticarial vasculitis from ordinary urticaria?
cards.answer: Lesions burn rather than itch, last >24 h (days), leave residual bruising/hyperpigmentation, and may have systemic features; it is a type III immune-complex process. Confirm with skin biopsy.

cards.text: {{AIRE}} enables thymic expression of tissue antigens (central tolerance); its loss causes APECED. {{FOXP3}}/Treg loss breaks peripheral tolerance and causes IPEX.
cards.explain: AIRE = central; FOXP3/Treg = peripheral.

cards.prompt: Match the tolerance-breaking mechanism to an example:
cards.explain: Four routes by which self-tolerance fails.

cards.prompt: What is the difference between autoimmunity and autoimmune disease?
cards.answer: Autoimmunity is recognition of self; autoimmune disease is when that recognition causes tissue damage. A positive autoantibody alone is not necessarily disease.

cards.text: CAPS is caused by {{NLRP3}}/cryopyrin mutations that activate the {{inflammasome}} and overproduce {{IL-1}}β, treated with an {{anti-IL-1}} biologic.
cards.explain: Innate autoinflammation, not adaptive autoimmunity.

cards.prompt: Contrast autoimmunity with autoinflammation.
cards.answer: Autoimmunity is an adaptive response (T cells/antibodies/immune complexes vs self). Autoinflammation is an innate response — defined mutations switch innate pathways on, with neutrophils and cytokines (e.g. IL-1) driving disease.

cards.prompt: Match the SLE lab to its meaning:
cards.explain: dsDNA + complement track activity; ANA is the entry test.

cards.prompt: Which two labs monitor SLE/lupus nephritis activity, and which is NOT followed serially?
cards.answer: Anti-dsDNA and C3/C4 complement track activity (rising dsDNA, falling complement = flare). ANA is sensitive but not followed to monitor response.

cards.prompt: An SLE patient on prednisone and mycophenolate spikes a fever. What is the guiding principle?
cards.answer: Infection until proven otherwise — immunosuppression raises and masks infection. Still consider flare, drug fever, thrombosis/vasculitis, and malignancy.

cards.prompt: Which are teratogenic and must be switched before conception? (select all)
cards.explain: Mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide, and methotrexate are teratogenic; hydroxychloroquine is generally continued in pregnancy.

cards.prompt: What is hydroxychloroquine's role and its key monitoring requirement in SLE?
cards.answer: Long-term immunomodulator that reduces flares (often continued in pregnancy); requires periodic eye monitoring for retinopathy.

cards.prompt: What do antiphospholipid antibodies confer in SLE, and a paradoxical lab finding?
cards.answer: Increased risk of thrombosis, pregnancy loss, and thrombocytopenia — yet they can paradoxically prolong the aPTT in vitro despite a prothrombotic tendency.

cards.prompt: Match the disease to its mechanism:
cards.explain: Graves stimulates, Hashimoto destroys; ITP targets platelet glycoproteins.

cards.prompt: Graves disease and Hashimoto differ in that Graves antibodies primarily:
options: Deposit immune complexes in the thyroid
cards.explain: Graves: stimulating TSH-receptor IgG → hyperthyroidism. Hashimoto: destructive → hypothyroidism.

cards.prompt: Match the infection pattern to the immune defect:
cards.explain: Infection pattern localizes the immune arm at fault.

cards.prompt: Order the initial immune work-up for recurrent infections:
cards.explain: Start cheap and broad, escalate to functional/genetic testing.

cards.prompt: Before diagnosing a primary immunodeficiency, what must you exclude, and why?
cards.answer: Secondary causes (HIV, immunosuppressants, malignancy, diabetes, malnutrition, asplenia, barrier defects, CF) — they are far more common than primary immunodeficiency.

cards.text: SCID presents in infancy with FTT, thrush, and PCP, and shows {{absent TRECs}} on newborn screen. It is an emergency: avoid {{live vaccines}} and arrange {{HSCT}}.
cards.explain: SCID = T-cell emergency; transplant is curative.

cards.prompt: How do CVID and X-linked agammaglobulinemia differ in presentation and B cells?
cards.answer: XLA (BTK): boys after 6 months, absent B cells, all isotypes very low. CVID: usually adolescents/adults, low IgG plus low IgA/IgM with poor vaccine responses (B cells present but dysfunctional), plus autoimmune/lymphoma risk.

cards.prompt: What does CATCH-22 stand for in DiGeorge syndrome?
cards.answer: Cardiac defects, Abnormal facies, Thymic hypoplasia, Cleft palate, Hypocalcemia — from a 22q11.2 deletion.

cards.prompt: Chronic granulomatous disease is diagnosed by an abnormal:
cards.explain: CGD = defective neutrophil oxidative burst → catalase-positive organisms and granulomas.

cards.prompt: Match the drug to its mechanism:
cards.explain: Calcineurin vs mTOR vs antimetabolites.

cards.prompt: What must be screened before starting an anti-TNF biologic, and why?
cards.answer: Latent tuberculosis and hepatitis B — anti-TNF therapy can reactivate them.

cards.prompt: Which adverse effects are characteristic of cyclophosphamide? (select all)
cards.explain: Cyclophosphamide: hemorrhagic cystitis, bladder cancer, AML, infertility. Retinopathy is hydroxychloroquine.

cards.prompt: Which biologic is used for CAPS, and which for refractory chronic spontaneous urticaria?
cards.answer: Anti-IL-1 (e.g. canakinumab/anakinra) for CAPS; anti-IgE (omalizumab) for refractory chronic spontaneous urticaria.

cards.text: Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine/tacrolimus) are characteristically {{nephrotoxic}}. Azathioprine causes marrow suppression, especially with {{TPMT}} deficiency.
cards.explain: Signature toxicities to recognize.

cards.prompt: What immunoglobulin/flow pattern distinguishes CVID from XLA?
cards.answer: CVID: low IgG with low IgA/IgM and poor vaccine titres, but B cells present. XLA: absent/very low all isotypes AND absent B cells on flow cytometry.

cards.prompt: How are C4 and C1-inhibitor used in the angioedema work-up?
cards.answer: Low/undetectable C4 with low C1-INH level/function supports C1-inhibitor deficiency (hereditary or acquired) — i.e. bradykinin-mediated angioedema.

cards.prompt: Eosinophilia is most associated with which group?
cards.explain: Eosinophilia points to allergy, drug reactions like DRESS, and parasites.

cards.text: SLE is a systemic type {{III}} disease: immune complexes deposit and activate complement, consuming {{C3/C4}}. Renal red flags include proteinuria, hematuria, and {{RBC casts}}.
cards.explain: Immune-complex nephritis with complement consumption.

cards.prompt: State a working clinical definition of anaphylaxis.
cards.answer: A rapid reaction with skin/mucosal plus respiratory or cardiovascular involvement, OR involvement of ≥2 organ systems after a likely allergen, OR hypotension after a known allergen.

cards.prompt: Give one infection consequence each for an innate vs an adaptive immune defect.
cards.answer: Innate (e.g. terminal complement) → recurrent Neisseria; adaptive (e.g. antibody deficiency) → recurrent encapsulated sinopulmonary infections.

cards.prompt: Why is a low/absent TREC newborn screen useful, and for what?
cards.answer: TRECs mark new T-cell development; low/absent values flag SCID early (a screening test, not perfect), enabling urgent treatment before infections occur.

cards.prompt: Before treating urticaria, what is the first thing to assess?
cards.answer: Rule out anaphylaxis/airway compromise — hives with respiratory symptoms, hypotension, or multi-system involvement need IM epinephrine.

guides.title: Week 11 · 1 Core Immune Framework
guides.html:
Foundations

Core Immune Framework

Week 11 · Immunology II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Innate vs adaptive defenses, B-cell activation with CD40L–CD40 help (and the hyper-IgM link), and the CD4 helper subsets with the infection each defect invites.

Innate vs adaptive

SystemComponentsDefects cause
InnateBarriers, complement, phagocytes, NK, pattern-recognition receptorsAbscesses, invasive bacterial/fungal infection, Neisseria (terminal complement), autoinflammation
AdaptiveB cells/antibody, T cells, antigen receptors, memoryVaccine failure, allergy, autoimmunity, rejection, most primary immunodeficiencies

B-cell activation & T-cell help

B cell binds antigen via BCR → internalizes → presents peptide on MHC II to a helper T cell. Linked recognition: T cell delivers help via CD40L–CD40 + cytokines → proliferation, class switching, affinity maturation, plasma + memory cells.

Hyper-IgM clinical link
Defects in CD40L or AID → hyper-IgM (high IgM, low IgG/IgA), recurrent encapsulated bacteria. CD40L also impairs macrophage activation → opportunistic infections (Pneumocystis jirovecii); AID does not.

CD4 helper subsets

SubsetCytokines / roleDefect →
Th1IFN-γ; macrophage activation; intracellular pathogensMycobacteria, intracellular organisms
Th2IL-4/5/13; IgE class switch, eosinophils, mast cellsAtopy, allergy, type I hypersensitivity
Th17IL-17; neutrophil recruitment, mucocutaneous defenseCandida, extracellular bacteria
TfhB-cell help in germinal centersPoor affinity maturation
TregFOXP3+, CD25+; IL-10/TGF-β suppressionLoss → IPEX, failed peripheral tolerance

guides.title: Week 11 · 2 Hypersensitivity & Allergy
guides.html:
Gell & Coombs

Hypersensitivity & Allergy

Week 11 · Immunology II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The four hypersensitivity types and how mechanism dictates testing, type I allergy and the anaphylaxis rule (epinephrine first, antihistamines/steroids are adjuncts), and the drug-reaction patterns by type and timing.

Gell & Coombs types

TypeMechanismTimingExamples
IIgE → mast-cell degranulation (histamine, leukotrienes)Minutes–hoursAllergic rhinitis/asthma, food allergy, anaphylaxis
IIIgG/IgM vs cell-surface antigen; complement/phagocytes; or alters receptor signallingVariableITP, AIHA, Graves, chronic spontaneous urticaria (anti-FcεR)
IIIImmune complexes deposit → complement activationSystemicSLE, serum sickness, urticarial vasculitis
IVT-cell delayed (CD4/CD8)Days–weeksContact dermatitis, morbilliform rash, DRESS, SJS/TEN
Mechanism predicts the test: IgE disease → skin test / specific IgE; type IV contact → patch testing; severe type IV drug reaction → stop drug + assess systemic involvement.

Type I & anaphylaxis

Do not miss
Anaphylaxis = rapid multi-system reaction (skin/mucosa + respiratory or cardiovascular, or ≥2 systems after a likely allergen, or hypotension after a known allergen). IM epinephrine into the outer thigh, repeat q5–15 min. H1 blockers and steroids are adjuncts — they do NOT reverse airway obstruction or hypotension.
  • Allergic rhinitis: intranasal corticosteroid + non-sedating 2nd-gen antihistamine; immunotherapy for selected aeroallergens.
  • Allergic asthma: ICS + bronchodilator; not all asthma is allergic.

Drug hypersensitivity patterns

PatternType / timingClues
Immediate / anaphylaxisI (or non-IgE)Min–hrs; hives, bronchospasm, hypotension (antibiotics, contrast, NSAIDs)
Drug-induced cytopeniaII; 2–7 daysThrombocytopenia/anemia/leukopenia → stop drug
Serum sicknessIII; 1–3 weeksFever, rash, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy
Morbilliform exanthemIV; days–weeksMaculopapular, no systemic involvement
DRESS, SJS/TENSevere IVFever, mucosal erosions, blistering, organ injury → emergency, stop culprit

guides.title: Week 11 · 3 Urticaria & Angioedema
guides.html:
Hives & Swelling

Urticaria & Angioedema

Week 11 · Immunology II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Urticaria vs angioedema, the five clinical scenarios from acute hives to autoimmune CSU and urticarial vasculitis, and the can't-miss bradykinin-mediated angioedema (HAE / ACE-inhibitor) that ignores antihistamines.

Definitions & first move

  • Urticaria: wheals/hives, sharply circumscribed, itchy, each lesion resolves <24 h (often 30 min–24 h).
  • Angioedema: deeper dermal/subcutaneous or mucosal swelling; burning/tightness > itch; resolves slowly (up to ~72 h).
First action
Rule out anaphylaxis/airway. Hives + respiratory or hypotension or multi-system → IM epinephrine.

The five scenarios

ScenarioMechanismManagement
Acute hives + clear triggerType I IgE / non-IgE mast cellAnaphylaxis → epi; else non-sedating H1 + avoidance
Acute hives + viral prodromeNon-allergic mast-cell activationNon-sedating H1; don't mislabel as food/vaccine allergy
Chronic spontaneous urticariaType II–like autoimmune (IgG vs IgE/FcεR)>6 wk, each lesion <24 h, no trigger; non-sedating H1, up-dose to 4×, omalizumab if refractory
Urticarial vasculitisType III immune complexBurning lesions lasting days, residual bruising, systemic; skin biopsy; immunosuppression
Angioedema WITHOUT hivesBradykinin / drugNo itch; abdominal pain; poor response to antihistamine/epi/steroid; check ACE-i, C4 + C1-INH

Bradykinin-mediated angioedema

C1 inhibitor deficiency / HAE
Recurrent angioedema without urticaria, severe abdominal pain or laryngeal swelling, family history, low C4 and low C1-INH level/function. Mediator is bradykinin, not histamine — so antihistamines/steroids/epinephrine don't work. Treat with C1-INH replacement, a bradykinin-receptor blocker (icatibant), or anti-kallikrein.

ACE inhibitor angioedema: bradykinin accumulation; lips/tongue, no urticaria; stop the ACE inhibitor (and avoid the class).

guides.title: Week 11 · 4 Tolerance, Autoimmunity & Autoinflammation
guides.html:
Self & Non-self

Tolerance, Autoimmunity & Autoinflammation

Week 11 · Immunology II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Central vs peripheral tolerance (AIRE→APECED, FOXP3→IPEX), the four ways tolerance breaks, and autoinflammation/CAPS — NLRP3 inflammasome → excess IL-1β, treated with anti-IL-1.

Central & peripheral tolerance

CellCentral tolerancePeripheral tolerance
B cellBone marrow: receptor editing or clonal deletion of autoreactive cellsAnergy (no co-stimulation); ignorance of segregated antigen
T cellThymus: positive (self-MHC) + negative selection; AIRE expresses tissue antigensAnergy; Treg suppression (IL-10/TGF-β)
AIRE mutation → APECED (central-tolerance failure, polyendocrinopathy + candidiasis). FOXP3/Treg loss → IPEX (peripheral-tolerance failure).

How tolerance breaks

MechanismExample
Enabling environment (inflammation/infection gives co-stimulation)SLE-like autoantibodies after an inflammatory trigger
Molecular mimicryRheumatic fever; reactive arthritis after GI infection
Access to hidden (privileged-site) antigensTrauma exposes eye/brain/testis antigens
Genetic susceptibilityHLA associations, AIRE, FOXP3
Clinical pearl
Autoimmunity = recognizing self; autoimmune disease = that recognition causes tissue damage. A positive autoantibody alone is not disease.

Autoinflammation & CAPS

AutoimmunityAutoinflammation
Adaptive; T cells / antibodies / immune complexes vs selfInnate; defined mutations switch innate pathways on; neutrophils + cytokines drive disease
CAPS
NLRP3/cryopyrin mutation → inappropriate inflammasome activation → caspase-1 → excess active IL-1β (no infection needed). Recurrent hive-like rash, fever, bone/joint pain, hearing loss; untreated → amyloidosis. ESR/CRP high; NLRP3 genetics. Treat with anti-IL-1 biologic (canakinumab/anakinra).

guides.title: Week 11 · 5 SLE & Autoimmune Diseases
guides.html:
Type III & Friends

SLE & Autoimmune Diseases

Week 11 · Immunology II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

SLE as immune-complex disease with its antibody/complement labs and activity monitoring, the management ladder (steroids + mycophenolate, hydroxychloroquine, pregnancy planning), and ITP/T1DM/Hashimoto/Graves.

SLE & lupus nephritis

Systemic type III hypersensitivity: anti-nuclear autoantibodies form immune complexes that deposit (skin, joints, kidney, serosa, CNS, vessels); complement activation drives inflammation.

LabMeaning
ANASensitive screen, not specific; negative argues against SLE; don't follow serially
Anti-dsDNASpecific; tracks nephritis / activity
Anti-SmHighly specific; poor activity marker
Low C3/C4Consumed in active disease; tracks activity
Antiphospholipid (anti-cardiolipin, LA, anti-β2GPI)Thrombosis, pregnancy loss, thrombocytopenia; prolongs aPTT
Do not miss
Fever in active SLE on prednisone/mycophenolate is infection until proven otherwise (also consider flare, drug fever, thrombosis, malignancy).
Monitor activity (esp. nephritis) with anti-dsDNA + C3/C4, not ANA.

SLE management

TherapyCaution
Corticosteroids (rapid control)Infection, adrenal suppression (taper!), hyperglycemia, bone loss, osteonecrosis, cataracts
Mycophenolate (steroid-sparing, nephritis)GI, leukopenia, teratogenic — switch before conception
CyclophosphamideMyelosuppression, infertility, hemorrhagic cystitis, bladder cancer
Hydroxychloroquine (long-term, reduces flares)Retinopathy (eye monitoring); often pregnancy-compatible
IVIG / plasmapheresisFc blockade / antibody removal; bridge therapies

Other autoimmune diseases

DiseaseMechanismKey
ITPType II IgG vs GPIIb/IIIaIsolated low platelets, normal PT/aPTT; steroids/IVIG if bleeding
Type 1 diabetesT-cell destruction of β cellsInsulin
HashimotoT-cell + anti-TPO → hypothyroidLevothyroxine
GravesType II TSH-receptor–stimulating IgG → hyperthyroidAntithyroid/RAI/surgery + beta-blocker

guides.title: Week 11 · 6 Recurrent Infections & Immunodeficiency
guides.html:
PID

Recurrent Infections & Immunodeficiency

Week 11 · Immunology II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

When to suspect immunodeficiency (rule out secondary first), the infection-pattern→defect map and initial work-up, and the primary immunodeficiency line-up — SCID, DiGeorge, XLA, hyper-IgM, CVID, CGD.

When to suspect immunodeficiency

Recurrent, severe, unusual/opportunistic infections, or failure to thrive. Rule out secondary causes first (far more common): barrier defects, CF/ciliary dyskinesia, diabetes, malnutrition, malignancy, HIV, immunosuppressants, asplenia.

Infection pattern → defect

PatternDefectTest
Abscesses, poor healing, catalase+ organisms, AspergillusPhagocyte/neutrophilOxidative burst (CGD); LAD testing
Recurrent NeisseriaTerminal complement (C5–9)CH50/AH50
Encapsulated bacteria + lupus-like diseaseEarly classical complementC3/C4, CH50
Candida, PCP, mycobacteria, severe viral, live-vaccine complicationsT-cellHIV first; T/B/NK flow; TREC (SCID)
Recurrent sinopulmonary (encapsulated), GiardiaB-cell/antibodyQuant IgG/IgA/IgM, vaccine titers
Initial work-up
History/exam → CBC + diff → quantitative IgG/IgA/IgM → vaccine titers (tetanus/pneumococcus) → T/B/NK flow → CH50 → HIV when cellular/opportunistic → specialized (oxidative burst, genetics).

Primary immunodeficiency examples

DiseaseDefect / clueManagement
SCIDProfound T-cell loss; infancy FTT, thrush, PCP, absent TRECs; emergencyAvoid live vaccines; HSCT ASAP
DiGeorge (22q11.2)Thymic hypoplasia; CATCH-22 (cardiac, facies, cleft, hypocalcemia)Manage cardiac/Ca; avoid live vaccines if severe
X-linked agammaglobulinemiaBTK; boys after 6 mo (maternal IgG wanes); absent B cells, all isotypes lowLifelong IVIG/SCIG
Hyper-IgM (CD40L)High IgM, low IgG/IgA; encapsulated + PCP/opportunisticIVIG/SCIG, prophylaxis, ± HSCT
CVIDLow IgG + low IgA/IgM, poor titers; adults; sinopulmonary, autoimmune cytopenia, lymphoma riskIVIG/SCIG, monitor autoimmune/lung/malignancy
CGDDefective neutrophil oxidative burst; catalase+ organisms, granulomasAntimicrobial prophylaxis, ± HSCT

guides.title: Week 11 · 7 Immunosuppression & Lab Cheat Sheet
guides.html:
Drugs & Tests

Immunosuppression & Lab Cheat Sheet

Week 11 · Immunology II
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The immunosuppressant classes by mechanism and signature toxicity (calcineurin nephrotoxicity, teratogens, anti-TNF TB/HBV screen), and a high-yield immunology lab interpretation table.

Immunosuppressant classes

DrugMechanismKey adverse / caution
CorticosteroidsGR → broad immune suppressionInfection, adrenal suppression (taper), diabetes, bone loss, osteonecrosis, cataracts
Azathioprine→ 6-MP; blocks purine synthesisMarrow suppression (esp. TPMT deficiency), skin cancer
CyclophosphamideAlkylatorAML, bladder cancer, hemorrhagic cystitis, infertility
MethotrexateFolate antagonistMyelosuppression, hepatotoxicity, pneumonitis, teratogenic
MycophenolateIMPDH / purine synthesis (T & B)GI, leukopenia, teratogenic
Cyclosporine / tacrolimusCalcineurin inhibitor → ↓IL-2Nephrotoxic, hypertension, infection
SirolimusmTOR inhibitorInfection/malignancy, metabolic, wound healing
BiologicsAnti-TNF, anti-IL-1 (CAPS), anti-IgE (omalizumab, CSU), anti-CD20Infection by target; anti-TNF: screen latent TB + hepatitis B first
IVIG / plasmapheresisFc blockade / antibody removalInfusion reactions; antibodies rebound without immunosuppression

Lab cheat sheet

TestHigh-yield read
CBC + diffNeutropenia → bacterial/fungal; lymphopenia → cellular; eosinophilia → allergy/DRESS/parasites
ESR/CRPNonspecific; usually normal in simple CSU
ANA / anti-dsDNA / anti-SmANA sensitive not specific; dsDNA tracks activity; Sm specific
C3/C4/CH50Low in active SLE; terminal defect → Neisseria
Quant IgG/IgA/IgM + vaccine titersLow IgG + low IgA/IgM + poor titers → CVID; absent all + absent B cells → XLA
Flow T/B/NK; TRECLow T → SCID/DiGeorge; absent B → XLA; low/absent TREC → SCID screen
Oxidative burstAbnormal in CGD
C4 + C1-INH level/functionLow in HAE/AAE (bradykinin angioedema)

guides.title: Week 11 · 8 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Immunology

Week 11 · Immunology
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Anaphylaxis → IM epinephrine FIRST (anterolateral thigh); airway, oxygen, fluids
Hereditary angioedema (swelling, NO urticaria; C1-INH) → C1-INH / icatibant — not epinephrine/antihistamine/steroid
ACE-inhibitor angioedema → stop the ACE inhibitor; airway watch
Asplenia / complement deficiency → encapsulated-organism sepsis risk (vaccinate, prompt abx)
Immunosuppressed + fever → low threshold for empiric antibiotics; think opportunists

Core framework Framework

Innate vs adaptive fast / non-specific vs specific / memory
B vs T cells antibody (humoral) vs cell-mediated
Complement opsonization, MAC, inflammation
MHC I (all cells → CD8) · II (APCs → CD4)
Antibodies IgM first, IgG memory, IgA mucosa, IgE allergy

Hypersensitivity Allergy

Type I IgE / mast cell → anaphylaxis, atopy (immediate)
Type II antibody-mediated cytotoxic (hemolysis, Goodpasture)
Type III immune complex (serum sickness, SLE, PSGN)
Type IV delayed T-cell (contact dermatitis, TB skin test, SJS)
Clue timing + mechanism separate them

Urticaria & angioedema Urticaria

Urticaria transient histamine wheals → antihistamines
Allergic angioedema with urticaria; mast-cell / histamine
Bradykinin angioedema HAE / ACE inhibitor; no urticaria
HAE low C1-INH / C4 → C1-INH or icatibant
Chronic urticaria antihistamines; omalizumab if refractory

Tolerance & autoimmunity Tolerance

Central tolerance thymus / marrow (delete self-reactive)
Peripheral tolerance Tregs, anergy
Autoimmune adaptive — autoantibodies / autoreactive T cells
Autoinflammatory innate — periodic fever syndromes
Trigger molecular mimicry, loss of tolerance

SLE & autoimmune SLE

ANA sensitive, not specific (screen)
Specific anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith
Multisystem malar rash, arthritis, serositis, nephritis, cytopenias
Flare low complement (C3/C4), rising anti-dsDNA
Base therapy hydroxychloroquine for most
Drug-induced anti-histone (hydralazine, procainamide)

Immunodeficiency PID

Encapsulated bacteria antibody or complement defect
Viral / fungal / opportunistic T-cell defect
Catalase-positive (e.g. Staph, Aspergillus) chronic granulomatous disease
When to suspect frequency, severity, unusual organisms/sites
Asplenia encapsulated sepsis — vaccinate + standby abx

Immunosuppression Therapeutics

Corticosteroids broad anti-inflammatory (infection, metabolic AEs)
Antimetabolites azathioprine (check TPMT), methotrexate (folate), mycophenolate
Calcineurin inhibitors tacrolimus / cyclosporine (nephrotoxic, levels)
Biologics anti-TNF (screen TB/HBV), rituximab (anti-CD20)
IVIG replacement + immunomodulation
Week 11 · Immunology · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.mechanism: α/β agonist (vasoconstriction, bronchodilation, stabilizes mast cells)
facets.monitoring: IM anterolateral thigh; repeat q5–15 min
Screen TB and hepatitis B before starting
